text:
This is how I’ve learned to use my own existence as the ultimate scientific “instrument”.

You can measure the the truth of any piece of knowledge just by introspection if you are willing to 1) accept all necessary pain and discomfort required to test that belief, and 2) let go of any particular attachment about your sense of self, and be open to anything that might happen.

The ultimate test here is death, because that’s the hardest thing to “pre-grieve”.

This is essence of what science is to me. An open, courageous attitude about the world. A love of creation.

text:
One of the most basic shifts in the global human OS coming is humans realizing that they don't control technology, and no one owns it.

4-500 years ago at the birth of Modernity, there was another shift in how Europeans related to nature that gave birth to modern technology as we know it.
It also gave birth to the current human OS, which is called science and which is a wonderful thing.

The dominant paradigm of Modernity is that humans control technology, and therefore own it. So we have copyright, patents, and capitalism as we know it.

But all of those old ways have been destabilized, and this is why our generation is so anxious about work-- we can feel that the way things are being done is not real, and there will be chaos.

Human beings cannot control technology, because knowledge is not objective-- it is fundamentally contextual and relational. LLMs make this obvious. All systems are wrong, some are useful. @Meaningness

LLMs are most useful when you care about what you are doing. If LLMs make you feel like your work is meaningless, then your work was always meaningless. It didn't feel that way because you thought your boss has A Plan. Your boss does not have A Plan.

There is no one corporation that can know how LLMs work. They cannot, therefore, own them.

Short and invest accordingly.

text:
I think there’s something even deeper here. Truth isn’t separate from emotion. I think that pain, tension, anger all point to areas where “we” collectively are confused. Idea I learned from @BrettPAndersen 

By clashing like this with the clusters of people on Twitter, you’re actively finding the collective “knots” where people are tense. You’re finding the areas where “we” are confused.

The super high iq move is to RELEASE the tension by saying something that is totally consistent with what both you AND they believe in a satisfying “Ahh” way. @BrettPAndersen hero’s journey

People don’t want to feel tense, they want to feel connected. When you show them a higher truth where they don’t have to be tense anymore, they join you.

text:
There’s a spectrum of transcendence here relative to where you are. The frontier and the backtier @DefenderOfBasic 

Sometimes you will say something that hits a nerve, gets pushback and you learn something. So you “let go” of the “weight” you were giving your previous belief, and update what you say, and now you belong. That feeling of belonging is a good thing.

But sometimes you hit a nerve and it’s because you are ahead of what your network or a part of it believes. So you “go back” like a hero and teach that network by marketing your more transcendent idea in a way that clicks. 

The reason why you can do this is that if you’re ahead you understand them better than they do, and you can speak their language. You can be the one who helps them “let go” of ideas making them feel tense and angry.

If love is epistemically necessary, then letting go of the weight we give to some beliefs is the way towards truth.

So you’re really on to something when you purposefully trigger people’s emotions.

text:
prediction: AI cannot do infinite work for you because you do not own AI. AI is most useful when you care about what you are doing, and AI helps you do what you care about more.

translation into $: there won't be big returns for scale, but instead a restructuring towards personalization. Less people will work for big companies, they will go home and they will start their own companies. Big companies won't see huge returns, just a restructuring. There will be chaos in the middle. ChatGPT is not useful for everything out of the box, it requires lot of effort put into context engineering, and that is where the value is.

But contextual knowledge is not transcendent and cannot put your questions to rest permanently

text: Love isn’t really moral but you can see it that way. Morality is annoying because it ignores the profundity of actually loving someone in this world of suffering and tries to fixate on habits rules or reasons, parts of the whole, as a way of avoiding the emotion of loving in the midst of all the suffering and chaos.

Morality is annoying because it ignores the profundity of actually loving someone in this world of suffering and tries to fixate on habits rules or reasons, parts of the whole, as a way of avoiding the emotion of loving in the midst of all the suffering and chaos.

text:
Reality breaks your conceptual models. Then you can act with love. But it’s not a mental decision. It’s made with the heart. Like physically with your awareness in the heart.

So love isn’t a moral stance. A moral stance is a mental thing. It’s knowledge, but love is before knowledge. You can love anything and everything in the world and act with love before you have any moral knowledge or stance.

You don’t really need moral knowledge. This is because reality will break any and every decision framework. It’s just not possible to cover all the edge cases. But you can literally love any situation, no matter how evil, and act from that place.

And once you look under the hood, all the control models are built from love, because “good” just happens to be our idea of where we have seen love in the world and want to preserve it.

Control is the opposite of surrender, and this is why conceptual knowledge is inherently limited. By trying to control an outcome in reality, you inherently close your eyes epistemically to some part of reality. So paradoxically fixating on some piece of knowledge inherently requires ignoring reality in some way. Surrender is letting go of controlling outcomes and being open to all outcomes happening including death at any time, so it’s fully accepting reality as it is. Epistemically this is the most open you can be to reality. It’s the same as loving everything and that’s why love is epistemically necessary, as @DefenderOfBasic says. But it also can’t be turned into knowledge like the control model you’re thinking of, because when you do that you fixate on a part of what has once been true and using that to try to control reality. By doing this you’re not being open to the possibility of the opposite happening in the future, and so no longer loving reality, loving everything.

You would think that it’s necessary to have a moral framework because there is a good that you are progressing towards or preserving. But that’s not true because ALL of reality is love including evil. You can love both good and bad situations. Reality will make you give up your control framework by putting you in situations where it can’t provide order anymore and breaking you, just to give you the opportunity to see one extra situation, part of reality, as love.

Because reality loves you and it’s actually conscious and it’s all god.

This is why Nietzsche said love is beyond good and evil. Why Defender says love is epistemically necessary. You can’t really see reality until you love everything.

But we need order. Order is not fundamentally real, it’s not fixable, you can’t control having it and nail it down. But it can be maintained constantly like gardening, until reality makes it not be through death, but that’s also love. And heroes who go into the unknown to seek knowledge to provide order from chaos are rewarded if they stick the course. That knowledge is valuable but you should never hold onto it. Never hold to order or always be good.

A lot of @Meaningness work is about finding new order new knowledge in the midst of chaos. But he doesn’t know that reality is conscious and that everything is love even though you can’t fixate it. That’s because he hasn’t considered the meaningness of death fully yet. If you surrendered yourself to the outcome of death, you wouldn’t go around trying to see meaningfulness and meaninglessness, you would just love everything because everything dies.

Go into the unknown be a hero and find knowledge to bring order. But I hope you also learn a way deeper more profound lesson along the way, and you let go of needing control, order or goodness.

Because everything eventually dies so none of it is real. That’s why everything is love so you can love everything.

text:
The totality of your models are your ego and if you live your life like a hero you constantly go towards the errors between your models and reality

Until you fully dissolve your ego and die in a spiritual sense

And then you can see everything as love as opposed to just some things in the world as love, which would have detectable features in your model

So love doesn’t really have detectable features or opposites because everything is love

If you haven’t yet seen everything as love, then you’re living in what the Hindus call Maya, or Samsara

The process of seeing everything as love, or just learning to love life from within and without, is what life is about

It’s THE process that’s always going on for everything including ants and egregores

Facing hardship is how you see everything as love because when you face hardship you change, so your models of the world change

text:
There’s basically 2 steps: courage + surrender

Courage is finding errors in your model of the world and trying to resolve them

Surrender is letting go of the rewards of your labor, which at the highest level is letting go of living a meaningful life, ie accepting death

If you do these things to a fault, 100% you will see everything as love, or heaven on earth

Greatest feeling in the world

It’s already heaven here, the only difference between heaven and your life is YOU

text: AI companies (data center + foundation models) will just be infrastructure companies with low margins in the medium term that everyone fights over politically. The foundation model building and training part is an expensive commodity that requires coordination and data and favors some centralization. It could have large margins thru rentseeking if there’s enough centralization so it’ll be like a political asset that everyone fights over. There won’t be enough centralization in the long term, but in the short term the American tech companies will be major flashpoints of conflict.

text:
Imagine a 24/7 app to find pediatricians where
1) you answer some questions about your child’s symptoms when they’re sick
2) get a quick response from a pediatrician letting you know what to do including after-hours and weekends
3) a same/next-day appointment if necessary

We know it’s hard when children get sick and you have work and we’re trying to learn from you to help solve this problem. :)
Help us design this service by answering a few questions below.

Demo link! https://t.co/husJpTjasp

This could be your current pediatrician, and you would have multiple options. Medical records could be shared with your permission. Messaging could have an out-of-pocket cost paid to the pediatrician, either $ per-month or $ per-response (includes multiple responses until issue resolved). The video/in-person appointments could be covered by your insurance if covered as usual or out-of-pocket.

We will also have an in-person urgent care option, where your records could be shared in advance at your request.

Help us design this app by answering these questions in the comments:
Would you use it if it costs $100/month to your pediatrician?

What about $20 per-response?

Would you use it if it costs $50/month to your pediatrician?

text:
There is huge difference between betting on AGI, free infinite slaves that automate most work, and betting AI will fundamentally reshape work in society.

I bet that AI is a bubble because the financial bet is on free infinite slaves owned by corporations but the reality will be the opposite, that massive bureaucracies themselves get outcompeted by AI-powered small flexible teams. Which is really funny. People will no longer have jobs, they will work from home.

Which is really funny. People will no longer have jobs, they will work from home.

text: My bet is that AI is not useful if you treat it like a slave, only if you care about your work. Which is really funny.

text:
Makes you rethink GDP. We have high GDPs per capita and disposable incomes spent using technology to access an immense amount of basic material choices in life, like traveling or using AI to learn Spanish.

But then what people really need money for, to foster relationships and grow families, has become so hard that lots of people now spend that disposable income seeking, breaking, repairing or replacing relationships.

It’s no wonder that the most expensive costs in American life are the industries of attachment— housing, education, healthcare, and government. These products are built on healthy relationships and foster attachments.

It’s only natural that as the price of TVs has fallen, the price of grandmas— a fancy word for nannies— has skyrocketed.
